content.text: What is a Private Cloud?

A private cloud is a cloud computing environment dedicated exclusively to a single organization. The defining characteristic is exclusivity — no shared hardware with other tenants. Resources may be hosted on-premises in the organization's own datacenter, or in a colocation facility, or on dedicated hardware that a vendor manages on the organization's behalf.

Private cloud retains the cloud operating model — self-service provisioning, elastic scale, automation, and metered consumption — while applying it to infrastructure that no other organization touches. The critical question is not "is it in my building?" but "is the hardware exclusively mine?"
Private Cloud Architecture

A private cloud has the same layers as public cloud — physical infrastructure, hypervisor, cloud management platform, and tenant isolation — but every layer is under your organization's control or your vendor's dedicated control.

attrs.alt: Private cloud architecture: left panel shows on-premises private cloud (physical layer → hypervisor → cloud management platform → self-service portal and automation → tenant isolation by department). Right panel shows Azure-managed private cloud options (Azure Stack HCI, Azure Stack Hub, Azure Stack Edge, Azure Arc).
attrs.title: Private Cloud Architecture Options

content.text: Why Organizations Choose Private Cloud
Regulatory and compliance requirements.

Certain data classifications — classified government data, certain healthcare records, financial data under specific national regulations — cannot legally reside on shared infrastructure. Private cloud provides the dedicated hardware required.
Air-gapped environments.

Defense, intelligence, and some industrial control systems operate in completely isolated networks with no internet connectivity. A private cloud can run without any connection to the public internet or a vendor's cloud.
Data sovereignty.

Some jurisdictions require certain data to remain in specific physical facilities under the organization's direct control, not just in a geographic region. A private cloud in an organization-owned building satisfies this requirement.
Existing capital investment.
Organizations that have recently purchased datacenter hardware with years of useful life remaining often build a private cloud on that hardware rather than immediately migrating to public cloud.
Predictable performance.

Dedicated hardware eliminates the noisy-neighbor risk entirely. Latency-sensitive workloads (high-frequency trading, real-time signal processing) sometimes require guaranteed hardware-level isolation.
On-Premises Private Cloud Technologies
VMware vSphere / VMware Cloud Foundation

VMware's vSphere (hypervisor) combined with vCenter (management) and NSX (software-defined networking) is the most widely deployed private cloud stack in enterprise datacenters. VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) adds VxRail hyper-converged infrastructure and an automation layer to create a complete private cloud platform.

For Azure customers, the integration path is Azure VMware Solution (AVS) — this extends VMware private clouds directly into Azure without re-platforming. VMs keep their VMware format; the Azure platform provides the bare-metal hosts.
Hyper-V and Windows Admin Center

Microsoft Hyper-V, included in Windows Server, is the foundation for Azure Stack HCI. Windows Admin Center provides a browser-based management interface for Hyper-V clusters, storage spaces, and SDN. This stack is the on-ramp to Azure Stack HCI and Azure Arc.
OpenStack

OpenStack is an open-source cloud management platform that provides IaaS capabilities on commodity hardware. It is widely used in telecommunications and large enterprises that require a vendor-neutral private cloud. Components include Nova (compute), Neutron (networking), Cinder (block storage), Swift (object storage), and Keystone (identity).
Azure-Managed Private Cloud: The Azure Stack Family
Microsoft offers a family of products that bring Azure capabilities to on-premises or edge environments while Microsoft manages the cloud platform layer:
Azure Stack HCI

Azure Stack HCI is a hyper-converged infrastructure (HCI) operating system that runs on validated hardware in your datacenter. It virtualizes compute, storage, and networking on a cluster of commodity servers, managed through the Azure portal and Windows Admin Center. Requires connectivity to Azure for billing and management but can run workloads when disconnected.
Key capabilities on Azure Stack HCI:
Run Azure VMs (same image format as public Azure) with Azure Arc integration
Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) on-premises — same control plane as public AKS
Azure Virtual Desktop (AVD) for on-premises persistent desktops
Storage Spaces Direct (S2D) for distributed storage with erasure coding
Software Defined Networking (SDN) via Windows Server SDN for VLANs and micro-segmentation

# Deploy a VM on Azure Stack HCI via Azure CLI (connected to Azure Arc)
# The cluster is registered and appears as an Azure resource
az stack-hci-vm create \
  --name "vm-app-01" \
  --resource-group "rg-hci-prod" \
  --custom-location "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-hci/providers/Microsoft.ExtendedLocation/customLocations/hci-cl" \
  --image "win2022datacenter" \
  --admin-username "azureadmin" \
  --hardware-profile memory-mb=8192 processors=4 vm-size=Custom \
  --network-profile network-interfaces="[{\"id\":\"/subscriptions/.../lnet-prod/logicalnetworks/lnet-10-0-0\"}"]" \
  --storage-path-id "/subscriptions/.../storagepaths/csv-path"

# List VMs on the HCI cluster (visible from public Azure)
az stack-hci-vm list --resource-group rg-hci-prod --output table
Azure Stack Hub

Azure Stack Hub is a full Azure environment in a rack you own. It runs the same Azure portal, ARM API, and a subset of Azure services (IaaS VMs, App Service, Azure Kubernetes Service, Azure SQL, Azure Functions, Key Vault, and more) completely on-premises. Designed for scenarios requiring:
Disconnected or intermittently connected operation (ships, remote military bases, mining sites)
Regulatory requirements mandating on-premises data residency while still using Azure-native APIs
Edge deployments where public cloud latency (100+ ms) is unacceptable
Developers write code once using Azure SDKs; the same code runs on public Azure and Azure Stack Hub. This consistent API surface is the key architectural advantage.
Azure Stack Edge

Azure Stack Edge is a hardware appliance (1U or 4U rack unit) that brings Azure compute and ML capabilities to the edge — factory floors, retail stores, clinics, ships. It runs Azure IoT Edge, Azure ML models, and Kubernetes workloads at the edge, then syncs data to Azure Storage.
The Pro GPU model includes an NVIDIA T4 GPU for real-time inference (object detection, anomaly detection) where data cannot leave the facility due to bandwidth constraints or privacy regulations.
Azure Arc: Extending Azure Management to Private Cloud

Azure Arc is not itself a private cloud — it is a management plane that projects on-premises and multi-cloud resources into Azure as if they were native Azure resources. This means you can apply Azure Policy, Microsoft Defender for Cloud, Azure Monitor, and Azure RBAC to servers, Kubernetes clusters, and databases running on your private cloud.

# Connect an on-premises Linux server to Azure Arc
# Run this on the target server — downloads and installs the Arc agent
curl -L https://aka.ms/azcmagent-linux | bash

azcmagent connect \
  --resource-group rg-arc \
  --tenant-id "<tenant-id>" \
  --location eastus \
  --subscription-id "<subscription-id>" \
  --cloud AzureCloud

# The server now appears in the Azure portal as a resource
# Apply policy: enforce TLS 1.2 on all Arc-connected servers
az policy assignment create \
  --name enforce-tls12 \
  --policy "/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/..." \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-arc"

# Connect an on-premises Kubernetes cluster to Azure Arc
az connectedk8s connect \
  --name k8s-onprem \
  --resource-group rg-arc \
  --location eastus

# Deploy a Helm chart to the on-premises cluster via GitOps (Flux)
az k8s-configuration create \
  --cluster-name k8s-onprem \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --resource-group rg-arc \
  --name app-config \
  --operator-instance-name flux \
  --operator-type flux \
  --repository-url https://github.com/myorg/k8s-config \
  --scope cluster
Azure VMware Solution (AVS)

Organizations running VMware on-premises can migrate to Azure VMware Solution without re-platforming. AVS provisions dedicated bare-metal Azure hosts (running VMware ESXi, vCenter, and NSX-T) inside Azure datacenters. From the organization's perspective, it looks and operates like an on-premises VMware private cloud — same tools (vCenter, HCX, vMotion), same VM format — but Microsoft manages the physical hardware.

# Create an Azure VMware Solution private cloud
# Minimum 3 hosts (one per AZ); scale in increments of 1
az vmware private-cloud create \
  --name avs-prod \
  --resource-group rg-avs \
  --location eastus \
  --sku av36     # AV36: 36 cores, 576 GB RAM per host
  --cluster-size 3 \
  --network-block 192.168.0.0/22  # /22 minimum for AVS management

# AVS uses ExpressRoute to connect to the rest of your Azure VNet
az vmware private-cloud show \
  --name avs-prod \
  --resource-group rg-avs \
  --query circuit.expressRouteID -o tsv
Private Cloud vs Public Cloud: Key Differences
Dimension
Private Cloud
Public Cloud (Azure)
Hardware
Dedicated to one org
Shared (logical isolation)
Upfront cost
High CapEx (servers, network, datacenter)
Zero CapEx (pure OpEx)
Elastic scale
Limited to owned hardware
Virtually unlimited
Ops burden
High (HW refresh, patching, cooling)
Low for PaaS/Serverless; medium for IaaS
Internet required
No (can be fully air-gapped)
Yes (or ExpressRoute)
Service breadth
Limited to what you build/license
200+ managed services
Compliance
Highest control; you own audit evidence
Microsoft holds certifications; you inherit them
Private Cloud Total Cost of Ownership
Private cloud's true cost is almost always underestimated by organizations that focus only on hardware acquisition price. A realistic TCO includes:
Facility costs:
Datacenter space, power, cooling (typically 1.5–2× hardware power for cooling overhead), physical security, and fire suppression.
Hardware refresh cycles:
Servers have a 3–5 year useful life. Storage arrays are often 5–7 years. Networking gear 7–10 years. Each refresh is a large capital outlay.
Operations staff:
Datacenter engineers, storage admins, network engineers, hypervisor admins. Typically 3–5 FTE per medium datacenter.
Software licensing:
VMware vSphere, vCenter, NSX, vSAN, backup software, monitoring tools — licensing costs rival hardware costs at scale.
Over-provisioning:

Private clouds must be provisioned for peak demand, not average. Hardware purchased for peak usage sits idle most of the time — typically 20–30% average utilization on-premises versus 70–80% on public cloud.
Common Mistakes
Treating private cloud as the default safe choice.

Many organizations choose private cloud from habit or inertia rather than genuine compliance or technical need. Public cloud with Private Endpoints and Entra ID satisfies most compliance requirements at far lower cost.
Not building the cloud operating model.

A private cloud is not just virtualization. Without self-service provisioning, infrastructure as code, and automated operations, you have a VM farm — not a cloud. Teams that skip the operating model gain little over traditional on-premises IT.
Underestimating HA complexity.

Achieving 99.99% uptime on-premises requires redundant power (dual PDUs, UPS, generators), redundant cooling, redundant switching (MLAG, EVPN), and storage HA (RAID, erasure coding). This is not free — it roughly doubles hardware cost and requires significant ops expertise.
Ignoring the burst problem.

Private clouds have a hard capacity ceiling. When demand spikes beyond what you provisioned, requests fail or queue. The solution is hybrid cloud — bursting peak load to the public cloud while keeping the baseline on-premises.
When to Choose Private Cloud
Private cloud is the right choice when one or more of the following are true:
The workload is classified at a level that prohibits shared infrastructure (government, defense)
The environment must be air-gapped (no internet connectivity)
A specific national regulation requires data to remain in organization-owned facilities
The organization has existing hardware investment with years of useful life remaining
At very large scale (>10,000 VMs with predictable load), the TCO of private cloud can be lower than public cloud — but this requires expert operations team
Mental Model

A private cloud is like owning a hotel versus booking rooms on Airbnb. When you own the hotel, every room is yours — no other guest can stay. You control the building's rules completely. But you also pay for empty rooms during off-season, hire all the staff year-round, fund every renovation, and deal with the boiler breaking at 2 AM. Airbnb (public cloud) lets you rent exactly what you need when you need it, at the cost of sharing a building with strangers and accepting that you cannot repaint the walls.
Private Cloud Networking Architecture

A private cloud's network fabric is the backbone that enables workload mobility, multi-tenancy isolation, and connectivity to external networks. Unlike a public cloud where the provider manages the underlay, you own every layer.
Software-Defined Networking (SDN)
Modern private clouds replace physical switches and VLANs with software-defined networking for agility and automation.
VXLAN overlays:
Encapsulate tenant traffic in UDP, allowing up to 16 million virtual networks (vs 4,096 VLANs). Used by VMware NSX-T and Azure Stack HCI.
Azure Stack HCI SDN:
Network Controller manages virtual networks, load balancers, and gateways. Defined in declarative JSON and applied via REST API.
BGP route reflectors:
Distribute routes between physical and virtual networks without static entries; essential for large spine-leaf topologies.
Microsegmentation:
Policy enforced at the vNIC level, not at physical ports. Each VM gets its own security group rules regardless of which host it lands on.
Connecting Private Cloud to Azure
Most enterprise private clouds connect to Azure for hybrid workloads, identity (Entra ID), and management.
Connectivity Option
Bandwidth
Latency
Use Case
ExpressRoute
1–100 Gbps
< 5 ms (typical)
Production hybrid workloads, database replication
Site-to-Site VPN
Up to 10 Gbps
20–60 ms (internet)
Dev/test, secondary workloads, backup channels
Azure Arc agents (HTTPS)
Control plane only
Minimal
Management, policy enforcement, monitoring — no data plane traffic
ExpressRoute Global Reach
Inherits ER
< 10 ms between sites
Site-to-site traffic routed through Azure backbone, bypassing internet
Security and Compliance in Private Cloud
Physical isolation is necessary but not sufficient. Private clouds still require layered security controls across identity, network, data, and the supply chain.
Defense-in-Depth Layers
Physical security:
Badge access, cameras, locked server racks, visitor logs. You own this in a private cloud — it doesn't come with a CSP SLA.
Firmware / supply chain:
Verify TPM attestation and Secure Boot on every host. Untrusted firmware can undermine all higher layers. Azure Stack HCI ships with Secured-core server support (DRTM, HVCI, VBS).
Hypervisor isolation:
Hyper-V Shielded VMs encrypt the VM state so even a compromised host admin cannot read guest memory. vSphere vTPM provides equivalent protection.
Network microsegmentation:
Deny-by-default ACLs at the vSwitch/vNIC level. East-west traffic between VMs is as controlled as north-south.
Identity:
Privileged Identity Management (PIM), MFA for all admin accounts, just-in-time access. Active Directory or Entra ID as the identity plane.
Data encryption:
BitLocker or VeraCrypt at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit, customer-managed keys in HSM (not software KMS).
Patch management:
Azure Stack HCI supports Azure Update Manager for orchestrated rolling updates. Unpatched hypervisors are the most common lateral movement vector in private cloud breaches.
Compliance Automation with Azure Policy (via Arc)
When on-premises servers are Arc-enabled, Azure Policy initiatives apply the same compliance guardrails you use in public cloud.

# Assign a CIS L1 initiative to an Arc resource group
az policy assignment create \
  --name "cis-l1-private-cloud" \
  --policy-set-definition "1f3afdf9-d0c9-4c3d-847f-89da613e70a8" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/on-prem-rg" \
  --mi-system-assigned \
  --location eastus

# View compliance results
az policy state list \
  --resource-group on-prem-rg \
  --query "[?complianceState=='NonCompliant'].{resource:resourceId,policy:policyDefinitionName}" \
  -o table
Operational Considerations
Capacity Planning

Unlike public cloud where capacity is elastic, private cloud requires advance provisioning. The typical rule: plan for peak plus 20–30% headroom so VMs can live-migrate during hardware maintenance without throttling.
N+1 compute redundancy:
Every cluster node's capacity must fit the remaining workloads if one node fails. For 4-node clusters this means each node holds ≤25% of total vCPU allocation.
Storage tiering:
Azure Stack HCI Storage Spaces Direct (S2D) automatically tiers data across NVMe, SSD, and HDD tiers. Monitor with
Get-StorageHealthReport
Refresh cycles:
Plan hardware refresh every 3–5 years. Hyper-V live migration lets you drain nodes to new hardware with zero VM downtime — script the process with PowerShell.
Burst to public cloud:
Azure Stack HCI Supplemental Package supports bursting peak dev/test workloads to Azure when local capacity is exhausted, billed at Azure PAYG rates.
Monitoring Private Cloud with Azure Monitor
Arc-enabled private cloud resources forward metrics and logs to a Log Analytics workspace — the same workspace used for public cloud resources.

# Install Azure Monitor agent on Arc-enabled Windows server
New-AzConnectedMachineExtension `
  -ResourceGroupName "on-prem-rg" `
  -MachineName "hci-node-01" `
  -Location "eastus" `
  -Publisher "Microsoft.Azure.Monitor" `
  -ExtensionType "AzureMonitorWindowsAgent" `
  -EnableAutomaticUpgrade

# Query CPU metrics via Log Analytics
# Perf table collects hardware counters from Arc servers
$query = @"
Perf
| where Computer == "hci-node-01"
| where ObjectName == "Processor Information" and CounterName == "% Processor Time"
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1h)
| summarize avg(CounterValue) by bin(TimeGenerated, 5m)
"@
Invoke-AzOperationalInsightsQuery -WorkspaceId $wsId -Query $query | Select-Object -ExpandProperty Results
Disaster Recovery for Private Cloud

Private clouds must plan for site-level disasters. Azure Site Recovery (ASR) replicates on-premises VMs to Azure as a warm standby, providing RPO minutes and RTO hours without a second private data center.

# Enable ASR replication for an on-premises VM to Azure
az site-recovery protected-item create \
  --resource-group dr-rg \
  --vault-name my-rsv \
  --fabric-name on-prem-fabric \
  --protection-container-name on-prem-container \
  --replicated-protected-item-name web-vm-01-asr \
  --properties '{"policyId":"/subscriptions/.../replicationPolicies/24h-rpo"}'

# Test failover — creates a non-disruptive test VM in Azure
az site-recovery protected-item planned-failover \
  --resource-group dr-rg \
  --vault-name my-rsv \
  --fabric-name on-prem-fabric \
  --protection-container-name on-prem-container \
  --replicated-protected-item-name web-vm-01-asr \
  --properties '{"failoverDirection":"PrimaryToRecovery","sourceSiteOperations":"NotRequired"}'
Hybrid Identity: Bridging Private and Public Cloud
Most private clouds inherit on-premises Active Directory. Azure enables hybrid identity so the same user credentials work in both environments without password sync exposed over the internet.
Microsoft Entra Connect:
Syncs on-premises AD accounts to Entra ID (cloud). Password hash sync (PHS) or pass-through authentication (PTA) for SSO. No ADFS servers required with PHS.
Hybrid Azure AD Join:
On-premises machines register with Entra ID without leaving the domain. Users get SSO to both on-prem resources and cloud SaaS apps (M365, etc.) from the same logon.
Entra ID Application Proxy:
Publish private web apps (intranet, legacy) to remote users via Entra ID pre-auth — no VPN required and no inbound firewall holes opened.
RBAC across Arc resources:
Assign Azure RBAC roles to on-premises resources projected through Arc. The same
az role assignment create
command that controls Azure VMs now controls Arc-registered servers.

# Install and configure Entra Connect for hybrid identity
# Run on domain controller or dedicated sync server

# 1. Download and install Microsoft Entra Connect
# (Interactive setup wizard — run as Domain Admin + Entra Global Admin)

# 2. After setup: verify sync status
Get-ADSyncScheduler          # Shows sync interval (default 30 min)
Get-ADSyncConnectorStatistics # Confirms objects synced

# 3. Force an immediate sync if needed
Start-ADSyncSyncCycle -PolicyType Delta   # Incremental changes
Start-ADSyncSyncCycle -PolicyType Initial # Full resync (slow)
Summary
Private cloud provides exclusive hardware — no tenant sharing — with the cloud operating model (self-service, automation, metered consumption).
Primary drivers: strict data classification, air-gap requirements, physical data sovereignty, and existing capital investment.
On-premises options: VMware vSphere/VCF, Hyper-V, OpenStack — you own hardware and operations.
Azure-managed options: Azure Stack HCI (HCI in your DC), Azure Stack Hub (full Azure API, disconnected), Azure Stack Edge (edge appliance for ML/IoT).
Azure Arc extends Azure management plane (Policy, Defender, Monitor, RBAC) to any private cloud or on-premises server/Kubernetes cluster.
True TCO includes facility, refresh cycles, ops staff, software licensing, and over-provisioning — often 2–3× the hardware purchase price.
